# Template_Performance — Lean 4 Performance Reference

A reference document for **performance optimization** in Lean 4 projects:
memory limits, build parallelism, tactic speed, profiling, and CI tuning.

This file is a commented template — not a buildable module.  Import it
nowhere; read it when a proof times out, a build is slow, or CI hits
an OOM.

## Audience
- Developers encountering `maxHeartbeats` failures
- CI engineers tuning Lean/Lake builds
- Anyone refactoring proofs for speed

## How to use
1. Search for the § section relevant to your problem
2. Copy the `set_option` or environment-variable snippet
3. Follow the checklist in §9 before merging

## References
* [Lean 4 reference — options](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/latest/)
* [Lake documentation](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/latest/Lake/)
* [Mathlib style guide](https://leanprover-community.github.io/contribute/style.html)
* [Lean 4 heartbeat profiling](https://leanprover-community.github.io/mathlib4_docs/)

## Tags
template, performance, heartbeats, memory, parallelism, profiling, CI, grind

```lean
-- This is a reference-only file.  The import below is commented out
-- because this file is not meant to be compiled.
-- import Mathlib.Tactic

set_option autoImplicit false
```

 ### §1  Memory and Resource Limits

```lean
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 1A  LEAN OPTIONS  (set in source files)
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- maxHeartbeats — the "CPU budget" for elaboration / tactic execution.
-- Default: 200,000  (= 40,000 × 5 multiplier).
-- One heartbeat ≈ one reduction step in the kernel.
--
-- USAGE: always use the LOCAL `set_option ... in` form so the limit
-- applies to exactly one declaration.
--
--   set_option maxHeartbeats 400000 in
--   theorem expensive_proof : ... := by ...
--
-- NEVER set `maxHeartbeats 0` globally — this disables the budget
-- entirely and hides performance regressions.  A proof that silently
-- takes 20 M heartbeats today will break when Lean or Mathlib changes.

-- synthInstance.maxHeartbeats — typeclass synthesis budget.
-- Default: 50,000.  Raise if Lean reports
-- "maximum class-instance resolution depth has been reached".
--
--   set_option synthInstance.maxHeartbeats 100000 in
--   instance : MyClass X := ...
--
-- If you need > 200 K, the typeclass graph is probably too deep.
-- Consider adding a shortcut instance or `@[reducible]` abbreviation.

-- maxRecDepth — maximum recursion depth for the elaborator.
-- Default: 3,200 (raised from 1,000 in Lean v4.27).
-- Needed for deeply nested macro expansions or recursive definitions.
--
--   set_option maxRecDepth 10000 in
--   def deeplyRecursive : ... := ...
--
-- Symptom: "deep recursion was detected" error during elaboration.

-- Summary of key options and their defaults:
--
--   Option                          Default    Scope
--   ──────────────────────────────── ────────── ──────────
--   maxHeartbeats                   200,000    per-decl
--   synthInstance.maxHeartbeats      50,000    per-decl
--   maxRecDepth                       3,200    per-decl
--   pp.maxSteps                       5,000    display
--   profiler                          false    per-decl

-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 1B  ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES  (set in shell / CI)
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- These control the Lean RUNTIME, not the elaborator.
-- Set them BEFORE invoking `lake build` or `lean`.

-- LEAN_THREAD_STACK_SIZE
--   Per-thread stack size in BYTES.  Default ≈ 8 MB.
--   Increase for deep recursion or large `decide`/`native_decide`.
--     export LEAN_THREAD_STACK_SIZE=33554432     # 32 MB

-- LEAN_INITIAL_HEAP_SIZE
--   Starting heap allocation in BYTES.  Default: runtime-chosen.
--   Pre-allocating avoids early GC pauses on large builds.
--     export LEAN_INITIAL_HEAP_SIZE=536870912    # 512 MB

-- LEAN_MAX_MEMORY
--   Hard cap on total memory in BYTES.  0 = unlimited.
--   CI runners should set this to avoid OOM-killing neighbours.
--     export LEAN_MAX_MEMORY=4294967296          # 4 GB

-- LEAN_GC_HEAP_LIMIT_GROWTH
--   Multiplier for GC growth factor (default ≈ 2.0).
--   Lower = more frequent GC = less peak memory.
--     export LEAN_GC_HEAP_LIMIT_GROWTH=2.0

-- LEAN_GC_STATS
--   Set to 1 to print GC statistics to stderr.
--   Useful for diagnosing memory spikes during builds.
--     export LEAN_GC_STATS=1

-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 1C  CI CONFIGURATION  (recommended shell preamble)
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- Recommended preamble for GitHub Actions / CI runners:
--
--   export LEAN_THREAD_STACK_SIZE=33554432   # 32 MB stack
--   export LEAN_MAX_MEMORY=4294967296        # 4 GB hard limit
--   lake exe cache get                       # download Mathlib .oleans
--   lake build -j$(nproc)                    # parallel build
```

 ### §2  Build Parallelism

```lean
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 2A  LAKE PARALLEL BUILD
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- Lake parallelizes compilation across MODULE BOUNDARIES.
-- Modules in the same DAG layer build simultaneously.
--
--   lake build -j8          # 8 parallel worker threads
--   lake build -j$(nproc)   # use all available CPU cores
--
-- Key principle: finer module DAG → more parallelism → faster builds.
-- If two modules are independent, they can compile in parallel.
-- If one imports the other, they MUST compile sequentially.

-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 2B  MODULE DAG AND PARALLELISM
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- Visualize your module DAG in layers:
--
--   Layer 0: [Tactics] [QualityGates] [PhaseClassification] [Profiling]
--            ↑ All 4 build in parallel (no interdependencies)
--
--   Layer 1: [CuspCatastrophe] [CCVGating] [EvaluationGaps]
--            ↑ All 3 build in parallel (after Layer 0 completes)
--
--   Layer 2: [PipelineAdaptive] [PhasePortrait] [StochasticCCV]
--            [ProvenanceChain] [ReinforcementLearning]
--            [AgenticSafety] [PipelineBypass] [FeedbackSemantics]
--            ↑ All 8 build in parallel (after Layer 1 completes)
--
--   Layer 3: [LyapunovStability] [CCVEContraction]
--            ↑ Both build in parallel (after Layer 2 completes)
--
-- More leaf modules at Layer 0 = more parallel work at the start.
-- Breaking one large Layer-2 module into 2 independent modules
-- doubles the parallelism at that layer.
--
-- ANTI-PATTERN: a single "Utils.lean" imported by everything.
-- This creates a sequential bottleneck — all modules wait for Utils.
-- Split utilities by topic into independent leaf modules.

-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 2C  FINE-GRAINED LIBRARY TARGETS  (lakefile.lean)
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- Use separate `lean_lib` targets for independent module groups.
-- Lake treats each `lean_lib` as an independent build unit.
--
--   lean_lib MyProjectFoundation where
--     srcDir := "."
--     globs := #[.submodules `MyProject.Tactics,
--                .submodules `MyProject.Foundations,
--                .submodules `MyProject.Classifiers,
--                .submodules `MyProject.Profiling]
--
--   lean_lib MyProjectDynamics where
--     srcDir := "."
--     globs := #[.submodules `MyProject.Stochastic,
--                .submodules `MyProject.Contraction,
--                .submodules `MyProject.Catastrophe]
--
-- This lets `lake build MyProjectFoundation` compile without touching
-- the dynamics modules, which is useful for fast iteration cycles.
```

 ### §3  Tactic Performance Hierarchy

```lean
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 3A  TACTIC SPEED TABLE  (approximate, varies with goal size)
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- From fastest to slowest:
--
--   Tactic            Typical Time   When to Use
--   ────────────────── ──────────── ──────────────────────────────────
--   rfl                   0 μs      Definitional equality
--   decide                1 ms      Small decidable propositions
--   norm_num              1 ms      Numeric computation
--   native_decide         varies    Large decidable (100–1000× faster
--                                   than `decide` on big types)
--   omega              1–100 ms     Linear Nat / Int arithmetic
--   linarith           1–100 ms     Linear real arithmetic
--   grind             10 ms–10 s    General-purpose SMT-based
--   simp only [...]   10–100 ms     Targeted rewriting
--   positivity        10–100 ms     Non-negativity goals
--   fun_prop          10–100 ms     Continuity, measurability
--   ring / field_simp 10–100 ms     Ring / field equalities
--   aesop            100 ms–10 s    Heuristic best-first search
--   nlinarith        100 ms–60 s    Nonlinear arithmetic (SLOW)
--   bare simp          100 ms+      Full simp set (AVOID in proofs)
--   polyrith           1–60 s       Polynomial arith (external oracle)
--   Duper              1–60 s       First-order logic prover

-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 3B  KEY PERFORMANCE RULES
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- 1. `simp only [...]` is 10–100× FASTER than bare `simp`.
--    Bare `simp` tries every @[simp] lemma in scope (thousands in
--    Mathlib).  `simp only` restricts to your explicit list.
--
-- 2. `native_decide` is 100–1000× FASTER than `decide` on large types.
--    `decide` interprets; `native_decide` compiles to native code.
--    TRADE-OFF: `native_decide` increases .olean size and uses `sorry`
--    axiom internally (Lean.ofReduceBool).
--
-- 3. `omega` is faster than `linarith` for Nat / Int goals.
--    Use `linarith` only when the goal involves reals or rationals.
--
-- 4. `grind` (v4.28) replaces many omega / linarith / simp chains.
--    It combines E-matching, congruence closure, and arithmetic.
--    Prefer `grind` for complex goals mixing arithmetic and rewrites.
--
-- 5. `first_par` (v4.28) — parser exists but NOT YET IMPLEMENTED.
--    See §6 for details. Use `first | t1 | t2` until available.

-- Example: replace a slow bare simp with targeted simp only
--
--   -- SLOW (scans full simp set):
--   -- theorem foo : ... := by simp
--
--   -- FAST (explicit lemma list):
--   -- theorem foo : ... := by simp only [Nat.add_comm, Nat.add_assoc]
```

 ### §4  Measuring Performance

```lean
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 4A  HEARTBEAT COUNTING
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- Use `#count_heartbeats` to measure elaboration cost:
--
--   #count_heartbeats in
--   theorem my_theorem : ... := by ...
--   -- Output: "used 42000 heartbeats"
--
-- Rule of thumb:
--   < 50 K heartbeats   → fast, no concern
--   50 K – 200 K        → acceptable, monitor for growth
--   200 K – 1 M         → slow, consider refactoring
--   > 1 M               → fragile, will break on toolchain update

-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 4B  PROFILING
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- `set_option profiler true` gives a tactic-level timing breakdown:
--
--   set_option profiler true in
--   theorem my_theorem : ... := by ...
--   -- Output: elapsed 0.523s
--   --   simp  0.312s
--   --   omega 0.004s
--   --   ...
--
-- Use this to identify which tactic in a `by` block is slow.
-- Often a single `simp` or `aesop` dominates the total time.

-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 4C  BUILD TIMING
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- Per-module compile times:
--   lake build --verbose
--
-- Total wall-clock time:
--   time lake build
--
-- For CI dashboards, capture the output of `--verbose` and parse
-- the per-module times to track regressions over commits.
```

 ### §5  Compilation Performance

```lean
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 5A  WHY LARGE FILES ARE SLOW
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- 1. ANY change to a .lean file invalidates ALL of its importers.
--    A typo fix in a 5000-line file triggers recompilation of every
--    module that depends on it — even if none of the exports changed.
--
-- 2. .olean cache is per-FILE, not per-theorem.
--    Lean cannot partially reuse a cached file; it's all or nothing.
--
-- 3. Large files take longer to elaborate even for small edits,
--    because elaboration is sequential within a file.
--
-- GUIDELINE: split files at ≈ 2500 lines.  If a file exceeds 3000
-- lines, it is almost certainly hurting your iteration speed.

-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 5B  IMPORT COSTS
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- `import Mathlib.Tactic` pulls in ≈ 500 modules.
-- For leaf modules that only need `omega` or `simp`, this is wasteful.
--
-- PREFER narrow imports:
--   import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Defs          -- ~10 modules
--   import Mathlib.Tactic.Omega           -- omega only
--   import Mathlib.Tactic.NormNum         -- norm_num only
--
-- `lake shake` (v4.28) identifies unused imports automatically.
-- Removing a single unused heavy import can save seconds per build.
--
-- ANTI-PATTERN:
--   import Mathlib.Tactic    -- 500+ modules, but you only use omega
--
-- BETTER:
--   import Mathlib.Tactic.Omega

-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
-- 5C  INCREMENTAL BUILDS
-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

-- Lake uses content-hash-based invalidation:
-- - Only recompiles modules whose source hash (or dependency hash) changed.
-- - `lake exe cache get` downloads pre-built Mathlib .oleans from the
--   Mathlib cache server — essential for CI (saves 30+ minutes).
-- - `lake clean` forces a full rebuild.  Use sparingly; prefer
--   `lake build` which does the right incremental thing.
```

 ### §6  first_par — Parallel Tactic Execution (v4.28)

```lean
-- NOTE: `first_par` syntax exists in v4.28.0 but the tactic is NOT YET
-- IMPLEMENTED. It will error with "has not been implemented". Use sequential
-- `first | tac1 | tac2 | tac3` instead until a future Lean version enables it.
-- When available, first_par will run tactics in parallel on multicore.
--
-- Syntax (use `first` until `first_par` is implemented):
--
--   example : P := by
--     first
--       | grind
--       | nlinarith [h1, h2]
--       | simp only [lemma1] <;> ring
--
-- This pattern is especially useful when:
-- - Multiple expensive tactics might work (grind vs nlinarith vs aesop)
-- - You're running on a multicore machine (CI runners with 4–16 cores)
-- - The proof is performance-critical and you want wall-clock speed
--
-- When `first_par` becomes available, swap `first` → `first_par` for
-- parallel execution. For tactics that each take < 10 ms, sequential
-- `first` will remain faster due to thread overhead.
```

 ### §7  grind Performance Tuning (v4.28)

```lean
-- `grind` is a general-purpose SMT-like tactic combining congruence
-- closure, E-matching, and arithmetic decision procedures.

-- PRE-REGISTER lemmas with @[grind] to avoid runtime search:
--
--   @[grind] theorem my_bridge_lemma : ... := ...
--
-- Registered lemmas are indexed at compile time and matched via
-- E-unification at proof time — faster than passing them inline.

-- PROJECT-SPECIFIC lemma sets via `register_grind_attr`:
--
--   register_grind_attr myproject_grind
--   @[myproject_grind] theorem ccv_bound : ... := ...
--
--   -- Use the custom set:
--   theorem my_proof : ... := by grind [myproject_grind]
--
-- This avoids polluting the global @[grind] set while still getting
-- the benefit of pre-indexed E-matching.

-- Useful grind modifiers:
--   grind +locals       — auto-include local hypotheses and let-bindings
--   grind +suggestions  — use library-level premise selection heuristics
--   grind +both         — combine +locals and +suggestions

-- Diagnostics:
--   #grind_lint check   — analyze your @[grind] lemmas for issues
--                         (e.g., non-linear patterns, redundant lemmas)
```

 ### §8  Project-Specific Performance Notes

```lean
-- Current project metrics (update as codebase evolves):
--
-- • maxHeartbeats usage: 7 occurrences across the codebase
--   - Range: 800 K to 4 M heartbeats
--   - 2 proofs at 4,000,000 (both in CCVEContraction.lean)
--     → Primary candidates for proof refactoring
--
-- • Large files (> 3000 lines): 3 files identified
--   → Split at logical boundaries for build speed
--
-- • Redundant `import Mathlib.Tactic` in 12 domain files
--   → Replace with narrow imports (`Mathlib.Tactic.Omega`, etc.)
--   → Expected saving: 2–5 s per affected module
--
-- • MyProjectFoundation lean_lib target correctly isolates leaf modules,
--   giving maximum Layer-0 parallelism.
--
-- • CCVEContraction is the build bottleneck (Layer 3, sequential).
--   Splitting its heaviest proofs into a separate module would let
--   the remaining proofs build in parallel.
```

 ### §9  Performance Checklist

```lean
-- Use this checklist before merging any PR that touches proofs:
--
-- □  `set_option maxHeartbeats` only per-theorem, never global
-- □  No bare `simp` — always `simp only [...]`
-- □  `#count_heartbeats` measured for any proof > 100 K heartbeats
-- □  All .lean files < 2500 lines
-- □  Narrow imports (no `import Mathlib.Tactic` in domain modules)
-- □  `lake shake` run to clean unused imports
-- □  `lake build -j$(nproc)` used for parallel builds
-- □  CI sets LEAN_THREAD_STACK_SIZE and LEAN_MAX_MEMORY
-- □  No `set_option maxHeartbeats 0` anywhere in the codebase
-- □  `native_decide` used instead of `decide` for large finite types
--
-- For proofs exceeding 1 M heartbeats:
-- □  Profiled with `set_option profiler true`
-- □  Slowest tactic identified and replaced or split
-- □  Intermediate lemmas extracted to reduce proof-term size
-- □  `grind` evaluated as alternative (`first_par` not yet implemented)
```
